title: COP26 Takeaways: Statement from Laura Diaz Anadon
summary: What was accomplished? COP26 created pressure for some countries to produce revised Nationally Determined Contributions, helped catalyze new pledges to reduce emissions (including from India), led to the approval of the rules to govern international cooperation and carbon markets (the Paris Rulebook) addressing the issue of double counting, and served as the Launchpad for some novel initiatives that could play a useful role. Among the announcements that were made, was one that recognizes the importance of strategic investment and government action to lower the cost of technologies to reach carbon neutrality.
body: As many have noted and expected, the Glasgow Climate Pact is a necessary but insufficient step forward. What was accomplished? COP26 created pressure for some countries to produce revised NDCs, helped catalyze new pledges to reduce emissions (including from India), led to the approval of the rules to govern international cooperation and carbon markets (the Paris Rulebook) addressing the issue of double counting, and served as the Launchpad for some novel initiatives that could play a useful role. Among the announcements that were made, I would first highlight one that recognizes the importance of strategic investment and government action to lower the cost of technologies to reach carbon neutrality: the Glasgow Breakthroughs (a focus in this report to which I contributed that was launched at COP26.) The Glasgow Breakthroughs involved over 40 world leaders and focuses on making clean technologies the cheapest available to facilitate their adoption around the world by 2030 in five key areas: power, road transport, steel, hydrogen, and agriculture. Combined with Mission Innovation and the First Movers Coalition, this announcement indicates that sectoral conversations and coordination may play a greater role going forward. This is important because ultimately we need to make decarbonization technologies across many areas cheap. The Global Methane Pledge was another positive development that expands the space for action. Initiated by the European Union and the United States, it now has more than 100 countries as signatories. But there is still a long way to go and time is of the essence. First, numerous assessments find that the national pledges, even if met (a big if), are still very far from delivering a 50 percent chance of achieving the 1.5 o C stabilization goal. In addition, they are often not backed by policies to deliver the short to medium term reductions needed. Saying "we will get to zero in 2050" in 29 years can be cheap talk. Second, the same lack of detail about how to deliver goals is also present in the Glasgow Financial Alliance for Net Zero (GFANZ) , which has emerged as a location for discussions about advancing net zero by financial institutions representing $130 trillion in assets. It is yet unclear whether or how it can lead to short- to medium-term concrete, transparent, verifiable, and significant investments to deliver net zero. Third, the lack of immediate significant progress in climate finance for mitigation and adaptation from developed country governments was, in my view, a major missed opportunity to build trust and credibility and to advance climate justice. Industrialized countries pledged in 2009 to deliver $100$ billion in climate finance by 2020 and this has not yet been delivered. This pledge should not just be met as quickly as possible (it may happen in 2022), but governments should go beyond the $100 billion. Statements and views expressed in this commentary are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, the Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. result.main_content_en: The Glasgow Climate Pact represents a necessary but insufficient step forward. Key accomplishments include pressure for revised Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs), new emissions reduction pledges, approval of the Paris Rulebook for carbon markets, and novel initiatives like the Glasgow Breakthroughs. The Breakthroughs focus on making clean technologies the cheapest option by 2030 in power, transport, steel, hydrogen, and agriculture, signaling greater sectoral coordination. The Global Methane Pledge expanded action space with over 100 signatories. However, significant gaps remain. Current pledges fall short of the 1.5°C goal and lack near-term policy backing. The Glasgow Financial Alliance for Net Zero lacks detail on delivering concrete, verifiable investments. Furthermore, developed countries' failure to meet the $100 billion climate finance pledge undermines trust and climate justice.
result.main_content_zh: 《格拉斯哥气候协议》是必要但不足的一步。主要成果包括：推动各国更新国家自主贡献（NDC）、催化新的减排承诺（如印度）、批准了规范国际碳市场及解决双重计算问题的《巴黎协定规则书》，并启动了可能发挥作用的创新倡议。其中，《格拉斯哥突破议程》尤为重要，它由40多位世界领导人推动，旨在到2030年在电力、公路运输、钢铁、氢能和农业五大领域使清洁技术成为最廉价选择，结合“使命创新”和“先行者联盟”，表明行业对话与协调将发挥更大作用。《全球甲烷承诺》也是一个积极进展，已有超100个国家签署。然而，挑战依然巨大。首先，现有承诺即使兑现，也远未达到实现1.5°C目标50%概率的要求，且缺乏中短期落实政策。其次，格拉斯哥净零金融联盟（GFANZ）虽规模庞大，但如何转化为具体、可核查的投资尚不明确。第三，发达国家未能兑现2020年前提供1000亿美元气候资金的承诺，错失了建立信任和推进气候正义的重要机会，这一目标必须尽快且超额完成。
keywords_en: Glasgow Financial Alliance for Net Zero (GFANZ)
keywords_zh: 格拉斯哥净零金融联盟（GFANZ）
